• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Compare the Estimations of Logit and Conditional Logit

Wei-Lun, Lin

2026-04-29

1. Setting of the Conjoint Experiment

The Choice Task In our experimental setting, we employ a forced-choice, paired-comparison design. A subject is presented with a sequence of independent trials (choice sets). In each trial, the subject evaluates exactly two mutually exclusive alternatives and is forced to choose their preferred option.

Attributes and Profiles Each alternative is defined by a specific combination of \(L\) attributes. To facilitate modeling, these categorical attributes are converted into dummy variables, resulting in a total of \(P\) parameters. Thus, any given alternative can be mathematically represented as a \(1 \times P\) feature vector \(X\). To prevent perfectly predictable choices, the experimental design ensures that the two alternatives within a single trial are never perfectly identical.

Random Utility Theory (RUM) The statistical modeling of the conjoint experiment is grounded in the Random Utility Model (RUM). RUM assumes that decision-makers act rationally to maximize their utility. The total latent utility \(U\) that a subject derives from an alternative is assumed to consist of two additive components: 1. Deterministic Utility (\(V\)): The observable portion of utility, modeled as a linear combination of the alternative’s attributes and the subject’s underlying preferences (part-worth utilities), such that \(V = X\boldsymbol\beta\). 2. Stochastic Utility (\(\epsilon\)): An unobservable, random error term capturing idiosyncratic preferences, unmeasured attributes, or decision noise.

Under this framework, a subject will choose Alternative 1 over Alternative 2 if and only if \(U_1 > U_2\). The goal of the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) is to recover the true preference parameters (\(\beta\)) based on the observed choices. Depending on how the probabilistic structure of the choice set is defined, researchers typically employ either standard Logistic Regression or the Conditional Logit model.

2. Mathematical Notations

Throughout the MLE derivation for the two models, we will use the following indices and variables:

  • Indices:
    • \(i \in \{1, \dots, n\}\): Subject.
    • \(j \in \{1, 2\}\): Alternative.
    • \(k \in \{1, \dots, K\}\): Choice task (trial).
    • The total number of observations (rows of data in long format) is \(2 \times n \times K\).
  • Variables:
    • \(y_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}\): The dependent variable. It equals \(1\) if subject \(i\) chooses alternative \(j\) in task \(k\), and \(0\) otherwise. Within a single task, the forced-choice condition ensures \(y_{i1k} + y_{i2k} = 1\).
    • \(X_{ijk}\): A \(1 \times P\) feature vector representing the attribute profile of alternative \(j\).
    • \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\): A \(P \times 1\) vector of parameters to be estimated.
    • \(\beta_0\): The intercept term, specific to the logistic regression model.

3. Models

We compare two models: logistic regression and conditional logit model.

3.1. Logistic Regression

The core assumption of standard Logistic Regression is that each row of data (each alternative) is treated as an independent Bernoulli trial. It completely ignores the mutually exclusive and competitive relationship between Alternative 1 and Alternative 2 within the same trial.

Step 3.1.1: Define the Probability Function

The probability \(\pi_{ijk}\) that an alternative is selected is the inverse-logit transformation of its absolute utility plus the intercept: \[P(y_{ijk}=1) = \pi_{ijk} = \frac{\exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})}{1 + \exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})}\]

Step 3.1.2: Likelihood Function

The likelihood function \(\mathcal{L}_{LR}\) is the product of the probabilities of all \(2nK\) independent events occurring: \[\mathcal{L}(\beta_0, \boldsymbol\beta_{LR}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{j=1}^{2} \prod_{k=1}^{K} (\pi_{ijk})^{y_{ijk}} (1 - \pi_{ijk})^{1 - y_{ijk}},\]

and the log-likelihood is: \[ \begin{align*} \ell(\beta_0, \boldsymbol\beta_{LR}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ y_{ijk} \ln(\pi_{ijk}) + (1 - y_{ijk}) \ln(1 - \pi_{ijk}) \right] \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left\{ y_{ijk} \ln[\frac{\exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})}{1 + \exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})}] + (1-y_{ijk}) \ln[{1\over 1 + \exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})}] \right\} \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ y_{ijk} (\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR}) - \ln(1 + \exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})) \right]. \end{align*} \]

Step 3.1.3: Derive the First Order Derivative

To find the maximum, we take the partial derivative with respect to the parameters \(\boldsymbol\beta_{LR}\) and \(\beta_0\) and set them to 0:

\[ \begin{cases} &\frac{\partial \ell_{LR}}{\partial \boldsymbol\beta_{LR}} = \sum_{i,k,j} \left[ y_{ijk} X_{ijk} - \frac{\exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})}{1 + \exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})} X_{ijk} \right] &= \sum_{i,k,j} (y_{ijk} - \pi_{ijk}) X_{ijk}, \\ &\frac{\partial \ell_{LR}}{\partial \beta_0} = \sum_{i,k,j} \left[ y_{ijk} - \frac{\exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})}{1 + \exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})} \right] &= \sum_{i,k,j} (y_{ijk} - \pi_{ijk}). \end{cases} \]

3.2. Conditional Logit

The core assumption of the Conditional Logit model (McFadden’s Choice Model) is that choice behavior occurs strictly within the boundaries of the Choice Set. The probability is founded on the relative comparison between the alternatives competing within the same trial.

Step 3.2.1: Define the Probability Function

Based on the Random Utility Model, assuming the error terms \(\epsilon\) follow an i.i.d. Type I Extreme Value distribution, the probability \(P_{ijk}\) that a subject chooses alternative \(j\) in task \(k\) depends entirely on its relative utility within the set: \[P(y_{ijk}=1 | \text{Choice Set } ik) = P_{ijk} = \frac{\exp(X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})}{\sum_{m=1}^{2} \exp(X_{imk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})}\]

Step 3.2.2: Likelihood Function

Within the same trial, the probabilities of the two alternatives sum to \(1\), and the subject must choose exactly one (\(\sum_j y_{ijk} = 1\)). Consequently, the probability contributed by the entire trial is simply the “probability of the chosen alternative.” The likelihood function \(\mathcal{L}_{CL}\) is the joint probability across all \(n \times K\) choice sets: \[\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\beta_{CL}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{j=1}^{2} \prod_{k=1}^{K} (P_{ijk})^{y_{ijk}}\]

, and the log-likelihood is: \[ \begin{align*} \ell(\boldsymbol\beta_{CL}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ijk} \ln(P_{ijk}) \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ijk} \ln\left[ \frac{\exp(X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})}{\sum_{m=1}^{2} \exp(X_{imk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})} \right] \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ijk} \left\{ X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL} - \ln\left[ \sum_{m=1}^{2} \exp(X_{imk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})\right] \right\}. \end{align*} \]

Step 3.2.3: Derive the First Order Derivative

Take the partial derivative with respect to the parameter \(\boldsymbol\beta_{CL}\): \[ \begin{align*} \frac{\partial \ell(\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})}{\partial \boldsymbol\beta_{CL}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ y_{ijk} X_{ijk} - \frac{\exp(X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL}) X_{ijk}}{\sum_{m=1}^2 \exp(X_{imk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})} \right] \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K}(y_{ijk} - P_{ijk})X_{ijk}. \end{align*} \]

3.3. Comparison Between Logistic Regression and Conditional Logit

As derivation above, the log-likelihood function of logistic regression is: \[\ell(\beta_0, \boldsymbol\beta_{LR}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ y_{ijk} (\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR}) - \ln(1 + \exp(\beta_0 + X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{LR})) \right],\] and that of conditional logit is: \[ \ell(\boldsymbol\beta_{CL}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ijk} \left\{ X_{ijk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL} - \ln\left[\sum_{m=1}^{2} \exp(X_{imk}\boldsymbol\beta_{CL})\right] \right\} \]

4. Simulation

4.1. Conjoint Experiment Design Setup

The simulation employs a forced-choice, paired-comparison conjoint experiment framework.

  • Choice Sets: Each choice task consists of exactly two mutually exclusive alternatives (Alternative A and Alternative B).
  • Attributes and Levels: The number of attributes, denoted as \(L\), is manipulated systematically as an experimental condition (e.g., \(L \in \{1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11\}\)). Every attribute is binary, possessing exactly two levels (coded as 0 and 1).
  • Orthogonality and Distinctiveness: The choice sets are derived from a full factorial design space (\(2^L\) possible profiles) with two strict constraints applied to the pairs:
    1. No Ties: The two alternatives within a single choice set are strictly distinct. Pairs with identical profiles across all attributes are excluded.
    2. No Repetitions: Each paired combination is unique. Profiles like (profile \(i\) vs profile \(j\)) and (profile \(j\) vs profile \(i\)) are treated as the same underlying choice set.
  • Position Bias Control: The left-right (or top-bottom) placement of the two alternatives within a trial is completely randomized with a 50% probability to ensure the baseline choice probability remains perfectly symmetric at 0.5 (yielding a theoretical intercept of 0).

4.2. Data Generating Process (Simulation Setup)

The synthetic data is generated based on the Random Utility Model (RUM) framework. To ensure robustness, the simulation utilizes a Monte Carlo approach with multiple random draws for the true parameters.

  • Sample Size and Trials: The simulation specifies \(N = 300\) subjects. Each subject completes \(K = 10\) choice trials. (Note: For low-dimensional designs where \(L=1\), the number of trials is automatically capped at the maximum possible number of unique choice sets to strictly prevent repetition).
  • True Part-Worth Utilities (\(\beta\)): To reflect realistic preference variation and to prevent algorithmic breakdown (e.g., complete separation caused by dominant alternatives), the true utility coefficients are drawn from a truncated normal distribution:

\[ \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \quad \text{bounded by } [-2.5, 2.5] \]

To marginalize out specific parameter-draw biases, multiple independent sets of \(\beta\) are drawn for each experimental condition. * Decision Rule: The total utility \(U\) of alternative \(j\) for subject \(i\) in trial \(k\) is formulated as:

\[ U_{ijk} = X_{ijk}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \epsilon_{ijk}, \]

where \(X_{ijk}\) is the row vector of attribute levels, and \(\epsilon_{ijk}\) is the pure random error term drawn from an independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) Type I Extreme Value (Gumbel) distribution. The subject strictly chooses the alternative that yields the highest total utility (i.e., \(Y_{ij^*k} = 1\) if \(U_{ij^*k} > U_{ijk}\), and \(0\) otherwise).

#' 向量化且極速的 Conjoint 模擬 (嚴格正交無平局設計)
run_orthogonal_conjoint_sim <- function(n_sims = 100, n_subj = 300, n_trials = 10, n_attr = 1, true_beta = c(1.5)) {
  
  # 1. 預先建立選項庫與配對庫
  profiles <- as.matrix(expand.grid(replicate(n_attr, c(0, 1), simplify = FALSE)))
  colnames(profiles) <- paste0("X", 1:n_attr)
  n_profiles <- nrow(profiles)
  
  valid_pairs <- combn(1:n_profiles, 2)
  max_possible_trials <- ncol(valid_pairs)
  actual_trials <- min(n_trials, max_possible_trials)
  
  total_obs <- n_subj * actual_trials * 2
  trial_ids <- rep(1:(n_subj * actual_trials), each = 2)
  
  res_cl <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sims, ncol = n_attr)
  res_lr <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_sims, ncol = n_attr + 1)
  
  f_lr <- as.formula(paste("choice ~", paste(colnames(profiles), collapse = " + ")))
  f_cl <- as.formula(paste("choice ~", paste(colnames(profiles), collapse = " + "), "+ strata(trial_id)"))
  
  for (s in 1:n_sims) {
    # --- 極速向量化資料生成 ---
    # 1. 一次性為所有受試者抽出 actual_trials 個不重複組合
    # replicate 會產生 actual_trials x n_subj 的矩陣，轉為 vector 即可攤平
    sampled_pairs_idx <- as.vector(replicate(n_subj, sample(1:max_possible_trials, actual_trials, replace = FALSE)))
    
    idx1 <- valid_pairs[1, sampled_pairs_idx]
    idx2 <- valid_pairs[2, sampled_pairs_idx]
    
    # 2. 隨機對調左右順序避免位置偏誤
    swap <- runif(length(idx1)) > 0.5
    temp <- idx1
    idx1[swap] <- idx2[swap]
    idx2[swap] <- temp[swap]
    
    # 3. 建立 Design Matrix (X)
    X <- matrix(0, nrow = total_obs, ncol = n_attr)
    X[c(TRUE, FALSE), ] <- profiles[idx1, ] # 偶數列放選項 1
    X[c(FALSE, TRUE), ] <- profiles[idx2, ] # 奇數列放選項 2
    
    # 4. 矩陣運算計算效用與機率
    V <- X %*% true_beta
    eps <- -log(-log(runif(total_obs))) 
    U <- V + eps
    
    U_mat <- matrix(U, ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)
    winner <- max.col(U_mat) 
    
    choice <- as.numeric(rep(c(1, 2), times = total_obs/2) == rep(winner, each = 2))
    
    # 組裝 Dataframe 以供模型配適
    df <- data.frame(trial_id = trial_ids, choice = choice)
    df <- cbind(df, as.data.frame(X))
    colnames(df)[3:(2+n_attr)] <- colnames(profiles)
    
    # --- 模型配適 ---
    fit_cl <- clogit(f_cl, data = df)
    res_cl[s, ] <- coef(fit_cl)
    
    suppressWarnings({ fit_lr <- glm(f_lr, family = binomial, data = df) })
    res_lr[s, ] <- coef(fit_lr)
  }
  
  # 彙整與計算 (Mean, Bias, Variance, MSE)
  mean_cl_raw <- colMeans(res_cl)
  mean_lr_raw <- colMeans(res_lr)
  
  # LR 的截距項真實值理論上為 0
  true_beta_lr <- c(0, true_beta)
  
  # 計算 Bias (估計平均值 - 真實值)
  bias_cl_raw <- mean_cl_raw - true_beta
  bias_lr_raw <- mean_lr_raw - true_beta_lr
  
  # 計算 Variance (跨模擬次數的變異)
  var_cl_raw <- apply(res_cl, 2, var)
  var_lr_raw <- apply(res_lr, 2, var)
  
  # 計算 MSE (Bias 的平方 + Variance)
  mse_cl_raw <- bias_cl_raw^2 + var_cl_raw
  mse_lr_raw <- bias_lr_raw^2 + var_lr_raw
  
  # 組合輸出 Dataframe，將 CL 的資料對齊 (截距項補 NA)
  results_df <- data.frame(
    Term         = c("Intercept", colnames(profiles)),
    True_Beta    = true_beta_lr,
    CL_Mean      = c(NA, mean_cl_raw),
    LR_Mean      = mean_lr_raw,
    CL_Bias      = c(NA, bias_cl_raw),
    LR_Bias      = bias_lr_raw,
    CL_Var       = c(NA, var_cl_raw),
    LR_Var       = var_lr_raw,
    CL_MSE       = c(NA, mse_cl_raw),
    LR_MSE       = mse_lr_raw,
    Ratio_LR_CL  = c(NA, mean_lr_raw[-1] / mean_cl_raw)
  )
}
if (file.exists("./2-res/0-simulation/simulation.rds")) {
  final_results_df <- read_rds("./2-res/0-simulation/simulation.rds")
} else {
  # 1. 建立更嚴謹的參數網格 (加入 beta_draw_id)
  sim_grid <- expand.grid(
    n_attr = c(1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11),
    # 在同一個 L 和 Beta_SD 下，我們測試 15 種完全不同的 true_beta 組合
    beta_draw_id = 1:15
  )
  
  cat("總共測試", nrow(sim_grid), "種情境組合 (包含多組獨立 Beta 抽樣)...\n")
  all_results <- list()
  
  for (i in 1:nrow(sim_grid)) {
    current_L <- sim_grid$n_attr[i]
    current_draw_id <- sim_grid$beta_draw_id[i]
    
    if(i %% 20 == 0) cat(sprintf("進度 %d/%d ...\n", i, nrow(sim_grid)))
    
    # 確保每次抽出的 beta 組合都不同
    set.seed(12345 + i) 
    current_true_beta <- rtruncnorm(n = current_L, a = -2.5, b = 2.5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
    
    # 執行極速模擬 (為了配合多組 beta，n_sims 設為 30 即可維持穩健性與速度平衡)
    res_df <- run_orthogonal_conjoint_sim(
      n_sims = 30, 
      n_subj = 300,
      n_trials = 10,
      n_attr = current_L,
      true_beta = current_true_beta
    )
    
    # 標記這是哪一組 beta 的結果
    res_df <- res_df %>%
      mutate(
        Scenario_L = current_L,
        Beta_Draw_ID = current_draw_id
      )
    
    all_results[[i]] <- res_df
  }
  
  final_results_df <- bind_rows(all_results)
  write_rds(final_results_df, "./2-res/0-simulation/simulation.rds")
  cat("系統性模擬完成！\n")
}

4.3. Result

4.3.1. \(\beta_{LR}\over\beta_{CL}\)

# 計算每一組「獨立 true_beta」在兩個模型參數的比值 (LR / CL)
final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>% 
  # group_by(Scenario_L, Beta_Draw_ID) %>%
  # summarise(
  #   # 利用迴歸斜率算出該組特定 beta 的縮水比例
  #   # Robust_Shrink_Ratio = coef(lm(LR_Mean ~ 0 + CL_Mean))[1], 
  #   ratio = LR_Mean / CL_Mean,
  #   .groups = "drop"
  # ) %>% 
  # --- 繪製 Boxplot + Jitter (觀察特定 Beta 組合的影響) ---
  ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(Scenario_L), y = Ratio_LR_CL)) +
  # geom_violin() +
    
  # 加入箱型圖，顯示中位數與四分位距 (IQR)
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  # 
  # # 加入散佈點 (每一個點代表一組完全不同的 true_beta 組合)
  geom_jitter(
    position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = 0.3, dodge.width = 0.8),
    size = 1.5, alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  theme_light() +
  labs(
    title = TeX("Ratio of $\\beta_{est}$ of Logistic Regression to Conditional Logit against Number of Attributes"),
    x = "Number of Binary Attributes (L)",
    y = TeX("$\\beta_{LR} / \\beta_{CL}$")
  )

Each point indicates one \(\beta\) value.

As we can see, when there is only 1 binary attribute, the ratio is 2. As the number of attributes increases, the ratio tends to decreases.

final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>% 
  # group_by(Scenario_L, Beta_Draw_ID) %>%
  # summarise(
  #   # 利用迴歸斜率算出該組特定 beta 的縮水比例
  #   # Robust_Shrink_Ratio = coef(lm(LR_Mean ~ 0 + CL_Mean))[1], 
  #   ratio = LR_Mean / CL_Mean,
  #   .groups = "drop"
  # ) %>% 
  # --- 繪製 Boxplot + Jitter (觀察特定 Beta 組合的影響) ---
  ggplot(aes(x = True_Beta, y = Ratio_LR_CL, color= as.factor(Scenario_L))) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", se=F, color="red", alpha=.6) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.6) +
  theme_light() +
  labs(
    title = TeX("Ratio of $\\beta_{est}$ of Logistic Regression to Conditional Logit Against $\\beta_{true}$"),
    x = TeX("$\\beta_{true}$"),
    y = TeX("$\\beta_{LR} / \\beta_{CL}$"),
    color = "Number of Attributes"
  )

The red line is the regression line of the ratio \(\beta_{LR}\over\beta_{CL}\) against \(\beta_{true}\), and the CI of the slope is \([-0.044, 0.022]\). Thus, there is no correlation between \(\beta_{true}\) and the ratio \(\beta_{LR}\over\beta_{CL}\).

4.3.2. Intercept of Logistic Regression Against Number of Attributes

final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term == "Intercept") %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(Scenario_L), y = LR_Mean)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  # 加入散佈點 (每一個點代表一組完全不同的 true_beta 組合)
  geom_jitter(position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = 0.15, dodge.width = 0.8), 
              size = 1.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  # geom_smooth(aes(x=Scenario_L), method="lm", se=F) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Intercepts of Logistic Regression Under Different Numbers of Attributes",
    x = "Numbers of Binary Attributes (L)",
    y = TeX("$\\beta_0$ (Intercept of Logistic Regression)")
  )

It seems that the value of \(\beta_0\) has nothing to do with the numbers of attributes (\(L\)).

4.3.3. \(\text{Bias}^2\) of Estimations

final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>% 
  mutate(
    CL_Bias = CL_Bias ^ 2,
    LR_Bias = LR_Bias ^ 2
  ) %>%
  group_by(Scenario_L) %>%
  select(c(Term, True_Beta, CL_Bias, LR_Bias, Scenario_L, Beta_Draw_ID)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=ends_with("_Bias"),
    names_to = "Model", names_pattern = "(.*)_Bias",
    values_to = "Bias"
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    Model = recode(Model, CL="Conditional Logit", LR="Logistic Regression"),
    Model = as.factor(Model)
  ) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=as.factor(Scenario_L), y=Bias, color=Model)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  geom_jitter(
    position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = 0.3, dodge.width = 0.8),
    size = 1.5, alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = TeX("Bias of the Estimation ($(\\beta_{true} - \\beta_{est})^2$)"),
    x = "Numbers of Attributes (L)",
    y = TeX("${Bias}^2$")
  ) +
  theme_light()

final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>% 
  mutate(
    CL_Bias = CL_Bias ^ 2,
    LR_Bias = LR_Bias ^ 2
  ) %>%
  group_by(Scenario_L) %>%
  select(c(Term, True_Beta, CL_Bias, LR_Bias, Scenario_L, Beta_Draw_ID)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=ends_with("_Bias"),
    names_to = "Model", names_pattern = "(.*)_Bias",
    values_to = "Bias"
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    Model = recode(Model, CL="Conditional Logit", LR="Logistic Regression"),
    Model = as.factor(Model)
  ) %>% 
  # lm(Bias ~ True_Beta*Scenario_L*Model, .) %>% summary()
  ggplot(aes(x=True_Beta, y=Bias, color=Model)) +
  # ggplot(aes(x=True_Beta, y=Bias, shape=Model, color=as.factor(Scenario_L))) +
  # geom_hline(yintercept=0, color="red") +
  geom_point(alpha=.6) +
  labs(
    title = TeX("${Bias}^2$ ($(\\beta_{true} - \\beta_{est})^2$) Against $\\beta_{true}$"),
    x = TeX("$\\beta_{true}$"),
    y = TeX("${Bias}^2$")
  ) +
  theme_light()

The squared bias of logistic regression grow as \(\beta_{true}\) moves away from zero, while that of conditional logit remain unaffected.

final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>% 
  mutate(CL_Bias = CL_Bias ^ 2) %>%
  group_by(Scenario_L) %>%
  select(c(Term, True_Beta, CL_Bias, Scenario_L, Beta_Draw_ID)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=True_Beta, y=CL_Bias, color=Scenario_L)) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", se=F) +
  # geom_hline(yintercept=0, color="red") +
  geom_point(alpha=.6) +
  labs(
    title = TeX("${Bias}^2$ of the Conditional Logit ($(\\beta_{true} - \\beta_{CL})^2$) Against $\\beta_{true}$"),
    x = TeX("$\\beta_{true}$"),
    y = TeX("${Bias}^2$"),
    color = "Number of Attributes"
  ) +
  theme_light()

Focusing on the squared bias of the conditional logit, there is no special patterns.

4.3.4. Variation of Estimation

final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>% 
  mutate(
    CL_Bias = CL_Bias ^ 2,
    LR_Bias = LR_Bias ^ 2
  ) %>%
  group_by(Scenario_L) %>%
  select(c(Term, CL_Var, LR_Var, Scenario_L, Beta_Draw_ID)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=ends_with("_Var"),
    names_to = "Model", names_pattern = "(.*)_Var",
    values_to = "Var"
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    Model = recode(Model, CL="Conditional Logit", LR="Logistic Regression"),
    Model = as.factor(Model)
  ) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=as.factor(Scenario_L), y=Var, color=Model)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  # 加入散佈點 (每一個點代表一組完全不同的 true_beta 組合)
  geom_jitter(
    position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = 0.15, dodge.width = 0.8), 
    size = 1.5, alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Variation of the Estimation",
    x = "Numbers of Attributes (L)",
    y = TeX("$Var(\\beta_{est})$")
  ) +
  theme_light()

final_results_df %>%
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>% 
  group_by(Scenario_L) %>%
  select(c(Term, True_Beta, CL_Var, LR_Var, Scenario_L, Beta_Draw_ID)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=ends_with("_Var"),
    names_to = "Model", names_pattern = "(.*)_Var",
    values_to = "Var"
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    Model = recode(Model, CL="Conditional Logit", LR="Logistic Regression"),
    Model = as.factor(Model)
  ) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=True_Beta, y=Var, color=Model)) +
  geom_point(alpha=.6) +
  # geom_smooth(method=lm, formula = , se=F) +
  labs(
    title = "Variation of the Estimation",
    x = TeX("$\\beta_{true}$"),
    y = TeX("$Var(\\beta_{est})$")
  ) +
  theme_light()

final_results_df %>% 
  filter(Term != "Intercept") %>%
  mutate(var_comp = LR_Var / CL_Var) %>% 
  select(c(2, 12, 14)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(True_Beta, var_comp, color=Scenario_L)) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, color="red") +
  geom_point() +
  theme_light() +
  labs(
    title = TeX("Ratio of Variance Against $\\beta_{true}$"),
    x = TeX("$\\beta_{true}$"),
    y = TeX("$Var(\\beta_{LR}) / Var(\\beta_{CL})$"),
    color = "Number of Attributes"
  )
